title: The Hidden Costs Colleges Don't Want You to Know About
subtitle: 大学隐性费用：低收入学生的真实困境

paragraphs.en: This fall, thousands of college students from across the country will begin their undergraduate studies at colleges around the nation. They will inevitably pack too much to fit in their tiny dorm rooms. They will also carry with them a huge student loan debt, in addition to countless hidden out-of-pocket costs paid for by their bank accounts and the bank accounts of their families.
paragraphs.zh: 今年秋天，来自全国各地的数千名大学生将在各地高校开始本科学习。他们势必会带太多东西，塞满小小的宿舍房间。同时，除了巨额学生贷款，他们还将背负无数隐性自付费用，这些钱都得从自己和家人的银行账户中掏。

paragraphs.en: At my well-respected, private, four-year university in Washington, D.C., which boasts a yearly tuition of $44,046 not including room and board, I receive over $57,000 yearly in financial aid. As a student from a family that is struggling to make ends meet, my financial aid package is a combination of federal grants and federal work study, university merit scholarships and financial aid awards, and about $8,000 yearly in federally subsidized and unsubsidized loans. On paper, my expenses and my financial aid just about even out. Off paper, they don't.
paragraphs.zh: 我就读于华盛顿特区一所声誉卓著的私立四年制大学，每年学费高达44046美元，还不包括食宿。我每年能获得超过57000美元的助学金。我来自一个勉强维持生计的家庭，我的助学金方案包含联邦助学金、联邦勤工俭学、大学优秀奖学金与助学金，以及每年约8000美元的联邦贴息与无贴息贷款。从账面上看，我的开支和助学金刚好相抵。但账面之外，远非如此。

paragraphs.en: Universities today are in the business of making money, and mine is no exception. They hit me right out of gate with a $160 fee to attend my freshman orientation, a price which does not include the cost of travel to and from the District. Almost every class has an associated fee not included in the cost of tuition, most between $40 and $100. Fees for lab science classes are the highest, and all students at my university are required to take at least one lab before they graduate. Buying a laptop proved a necessity and, thankfully, a relative bought me one as a gift. Renting a mini-fridge for my dorm room costs my roommates and me about $140 a year.
paragraphs.zh: 如今的大学本身就是一门赚钱的生意，我的学校也不例外。一入学，他们就收了我160美元的新生迎新费，这还不包括往返华盛顿特区的交通费。几乎每门课都有不包含在学费内的额外费用，大多在40到100美元之间。实验科学课的费用最高，而我校所有学生毕业前都必须至少修一门实验课。买笔记本电脑是必需的，幸好有亲戚买了一台送我。租一台宿舍用的迷你冰箱，我和室友每年得花大约140美元。

paragraphs.en: Schools will charge you whatever they can. The costs of any damage to the dorm, including elevators, bathrooms, and common areas, are billed to every person on a dorm floor, or even the entire building if they do not know who caused the damage. After I fell out of my bed twice during my freshman year, the university installed a railing — for $20, billed to my student account. My financial aid did not anticipate any of these costs, and so it did not cover them.
paragraphs.zh: 学校会想尽办法向你收费。宿舍的任何损坏，包括电梯、卫生间和公共区域，其修理费都会摊派给该楼层每个人；如果查不出是谁干的，甚至会向整栋楼的学生收费。我大一时从床上摔下来两次，之后学校给床装了护栏——收费20美元，记在我的学生账户上。我的助学金没有预料到这些开支，所以也不予报销。

paragraphs.en: An Internet search of hidden costs of college turns up a host of articles on parent-centered websites on the college application process. These articles are almost always geared towards upper- and middle-class families. For students already struggling to pay tuition, these costs may be the least of their worries.
paragraphs.zh: 在网上搜索大学的隐性费用，会出现大量针对家长的关于大学申请流程的文章。这些文章几乎无一例外地面向中上阶层家庭。对于那些连学费都难以负担的学生来说，这些额外费用或许是最不值得担心的事了。

paragraphs.en: If you are a low-income student who will be attending school out-of-state, make sure you know if you can use your state benefits, such as the Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, or SNAP. It wasn't until after I had accepted admission to an out-of-state school that I learned that I could not use my Ohio Medicaid on campus for anything other than emergency care. My benefits became invalid the second I moved out of Ohio. After my freshman year, I had to opt for the school's insurance plan, which costs around $2,000 a year. Even if your school offers a flat-rate fee for a doctor's appointment at the student health center, these fees often do not include extra fees for lab tests or prescription medications.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你是一名要去外州上学的低收入学生，请务必确认能否使用本州的福利，比如补充营养援助计划(SNAP)。我是在接受了一所外州学校的录取之后，才知道我在俄亥俄州的医疗补助在校园里只能用于急诊。我一搬出俄亥俄州，那些福利就失效了。大一结束后，我不得不购买学校的保险计划，每年大约2000美元。即便学校健康中心的挂号费是固定的（我这里是每次20美元），这笔费用通常也不包含化验或处方药的额外花费。

paragraphs.en: If you plan on paying off bills in your student account with a credit card, be aware of any additional costs. My school charges an additional fee for the use of a credit card to settle outstanding charges, which can add upwards of 3% of the balance to your bill.
paragraphs.zh: 如果你打算用信用卡支付学生账户里的账单，要留心额外费用。我的学校对使用信用卡结清欠款会加收手续费，最多可达欠款余额的3%。

paragraphs.en: There is another depressing reality hidden within even the largest financial aid packages: Colleges often offer the most generous packages during freshman year as a way to attract new students. My family was careful to ask about the chances of financial aid being taken away after my freshman year. We were assured that, except for low grades or a raise in family income, no money would be taken away. We did not know to ask — and the school did not readily point out — that even if tuition rises, my financial aid package will stay the same. So when my university voted to raise tuition costs 3% at the end of my freshman year, my financial aid package remained the same and I was suddenly responsible for an additional $1,200 for the next year. The university administration will likely vote to raise costs at least once more before my graduation.
paragraphs.zh: 即便在最慷慨的助学金方案中，也隐藏着另一个令人沮丧的现实：大学通常在大一提供最丰厚的资助以吸引新生。我家人很谨慎地询问过大一之后助学金被取消的可能性。我们得到的保证是，除非成绩下滑或家庭收入增加，否则助学金不会被削减。我们当时不知道要问——学校也没有主动说明——即便学费上涨，助学金金额也维持不变。因此，当我的学校在大一结束时投票将学费上调3%后，我的助学金仍原地不动，下一年我突然就要多承担1200美元。在我毕业之前，校方很可能还会再次投票涨费。

paragraphs.en: Yes, I chose to attend an expensive university far from my hometown. Yes, there were cheaper options. But there are promising students from struggling families across the nation who should not rule out their dream schools entirely. All things considered, I am paying significantly less than the ticket price of my university, and having an educational experience in Washington, D.C., that I would not have had anywhere else. As a low-income student from a down-and-out Rust Belt community, these educational experiences have enormous potential to brighten my future — and my family's future.
paragraphs.zh: 是的，我选择了一所离家很远且学费昂贵的大学。是的，也有更便宜的选择。但全国各地有许多来自困难家庭、富有潜力的学生，他们不该完全放弃自己的理想院校。总体来看，我实际支付的学费比学校标价低得多，而且我在华盛顿特区获得的教育体验是别处无法给予的。作为一名来自衰败的锈带社区的低收入学生，这些教育经历蕴含着巨大的潜力，足以照亮我的未来——也照亮我家庭的未来。

words.meaning: adj. 穷困潦倒的；落魄的

paragraphs.en: The key is to make sure that students and their families understand that hidden costs exist, and that they may prove problematic. Fill out a more comprehensive checklist, and be wary of listed prices that seem too low. Understand just how complicated the financial aid process is.
paragraphs.zh: 关键在于，要确保学生及其家庭认识到隐性费用确实存在，而且可能会造成麻烦。请填写一份更全面的费用清单，并警惕那些看似过低的标价。要明白助学金申请流程究竟有多复杂。

paragraphs.en: Students and families must also understand their ability to self-advocate. They should not pay student bills or excess fees blindly. If something does not look right, ask about it. If it still doesn't look right, negotiate it. In cases where parents are working multiple jobs, are less knowledgeable about college bureaucracies, have limited English language skills, or are not contributing financially to their child's education, the burden of self-advocacy will fall on the student. I understand the difficulty, and the embarrassment. But it is necessary.
paragraphs.zh: 学生和家庭还必须认识到自我争取权益的能力。不要盲目支付学生账单或额外费用。如果有什么不对劲，就去询问。如果仍觉得不合理，就去协商。当父母身兼数职、对高校官僚体系知之甚少、英语能力有限或无法提供经济支持时，自我争取的重任就会落到学生肩上。我理解这其中的困难与窘迫，但这一步非走不可。

words.meaning: v. 为自己争取权益；自我倡导

paragraphs.en: In the grand scheme of things, however, colleges also must come to understand that the hidden fees they ask for may prove unmanageable for the very kinds of low-income or first-generation students they are trying desperately to attract.
paragraphs.zh: 然而从大局来看，大学也必须认识到，他们所要求的那些隐性费用，对于他们正拼命吸引的低收入学生或第一代大学生来说，可能是难以承受的负担。

words.meaning: adj. 难以处理的；无法管理的
